text: To reveal the cracking mechanism of wharf pile foundations under the condition of siltation on the bank slope, the current siltation status of the bank slopes of 16 major coastal ports in Jiangsu and Zhejiang regions and the cracking and damage of pile foundations of 7 typical wharves were investigated. Combined with specific projects, a finite element model of the interaction between the silted bank slope and the wharf pile foundation was established, and the force-deformation characteristics of the pile-soil system were analyzed. The plastic deformation state of the pile foundation was determined by the combination value of internal forces, and the result was basically consistent with the actual position of the cracking axis of the wharf pile foundation, verifying the rationality of the modeling. A total of seven factors, namely the engineering geological conditions of the bank slope and the structural parameters of the high-pile wharf, were selected for orthogonal experiments. The range analysis method was adopted to explore the sensitivity of each factor to the internal forces of the pile foundation. The results show that in the Marine environment, sediment constantly accumulates beneath and behind the pile foundation wharf, and the continuous deformation of the Marine soft soil layer causes passive pile problems. That is, under the condition of silting on the bank slope, the soil beneath the wharf moves significantly towards the sea side, and the adjacent pile body is squeezed to produce a large horizontal displacement, resulting in a sharp increase in the axial force and bending moment of the pile foundation. This further induces the top of some pile foundations or the areas with the maximum displacement to exceed the plastic deformation limit. During the siltation process, the additional internal force on the shore inclined piles at the rear of the wharf is greater, making them more prone to cracking and damage. Under the condition of siltation on the bank slope, the axial force at the top of the wharf pile and the bending moment are relatively sensitive to the structural parameters and the slope of the bank slope.

(1) The development and maintenance of the deep pools in the remote mountains are attributed to the unique topography, hydrology and other conditions of this sea area (for details, see 5). Section 2) The result of the combined effect. The land reclamation project from the sea and the siltation environment around the southern shore of Hangzhou Bay have changed the topography and water flow and sediment conditions of the surrounding area, causing the deep mountains and ponds that the Haigang District of Ningbo Port Town relies on for survival to gradually shrink and become shallower. Since 1995, the -30 m contour line of Shentan has disappeared. By 2008, the -20 m contour line had vanished, and the -15 m contour line also shrank significantly to the east. The rapid siltation of the deep pools in the remote mountains is a response to the changes in the topography and hydrological conditions of the sea area caused by human activities. (2) The reasons for the siltation of the deep pool on the outer mountain, which is the basis for the survival of the Zhenhai Port, over the past 40 years were sorted out. The contribution of different factors to the siltation of the port area was distinguished. Among them, the large-scale surrounding enclosure and the local enclosure of the outer mountain significantly weakened the rock head effect, which was the main reason for the continuous siltation of this deep pool. (3) When implementing coastal engineering projects, comprehensive planning must be carried out and the scheme with the best overall benefits should be selected. As the scale of utilization of China's coastline expands, conflicts among similar projects will continue to arise. To address this issue, it is necessary to formulate comprehensive development and construction plans and constantly coordinate, revise and improve them. Only in this way can the sustainability of coastal zone development be guaranteed.

Sediment transport is often the result of the combined action of waves or water flow. Water flow can erode the seabed and coast, or carry silt and sediment from elsewhere. Waves can erode the coast to produce sediment, or stir up the sediment in shallow shoals, allowing wave currents or ocean currents to carry it away [3]. According to relevant research [2], there is a net sediment transport from Hangzhou Bay in the sea area near Zhenhai Port: during the high tide period, sediment from Hangzhou Bay enters this sea area with the falling tide, and its quantity value can reach 0 in one tide cycle. 01.1 billion tons; The direction and quantity of net sediment discharge during medium tide are basically the same as those during high tide. During the low tide period, the net sediment transport direction is opposite. Sediment enters the interior of Hangzhou Bay from this sea area, and its value reaches 0. 0.094 billion tons. For the net inflow and outflow volume, it is the transfer from the Zhenhai Sea area to the interior of Hangzhou Bay. The completely different directions of net sediment transfer and net water transfer reflect the complexity of the seabed evolution in the Zhenhai Sea area. The seabed sediment types in this sea area are all clayey silt, among which the sand content accounts for approximately 1. 15%, silt content accounts for approximately 59%, and clay content accounts for about 39%. 8%. According to the sediment survey, the distribution of sediments in this sea area has the following characteristics: longitudinally, from south to north, the particle size of the sediments gradually becomes finer. Horizontally, the sediment particles in the middle of the Huibei Ocean are slightly coarser while those on both sides are finer. The distribution characteristics of this sediment are the result of the strong tidal current of the Jintang Waterway and the concealment effect of the islands.

The basic appearance of erosion and siltation: The inflow volume of Fuhong enables the Jintang waterway to maintain a water depth of 70 to 80 meters. The rising and falling tides closely follow the shorelines on both sides of the Yongjiang River estuary and flow back and forth in the deep trough area. Therefore, the shoreline of Zhenhai has been relatively stable for a long time. Based on the 1:50,000 underwater topographic survey map from 1928 to 2002, the distribution of erosion and siltation in this sea area was analyzed (Figure 3). With the Yongjiang Estuary as the boundary, the erosion and siltation patterns on the east and west sides are different. The east side generally shows a weak erosion, while the area west of the Yongjiang Estuary where the Zhenhai Port District is located shows a slow siltation trend. Over the past century or so, the overall location of the deep trough has remained largely unchanged. The contour lines of the Jintang Waterway and the deep trough along the west side of Jintang Mountain have also changed little. However, the waters of the deep pools outside the Yongjiang Estuary have undergone significant changes, mainly manifested as siltation. Due to the influence of human activities such as siltation on the periphery of the southern bank of Hangzhou Bay and nearby reclamation projects, the deep trough has been silted up year by year. After 1995, the -30 m contour line disappeared. By 2008, the -20 m contour line had vanished, and the -15 m contour line was significantly silted towards the Jintang Waterway (approximately 2). There is only a local deep pool at an elevation of -15 meters at the top of the mountain outside the area, which is 8 kilometers long.

The development and maintenance of the deep pools in the outer mountains are mainly influenced by two factors: tidal current and topography. 1. The abundant inflow of the Jintang waterway enables it to maintain a water depth of 70 to 80 meters. The rising and falling tidal currents closely follow the shorelines on both sides of the Yongjiang River estuary and flow back and forth in the deep trough area. Meanwhile, the falling tidal current of Hangzhou Bay from north to south is constrained by the shorelines, and the tidal current strengthens near the outer mountains. The coastline of the two towns runs from northwest to southeast. The strong downcurrent is blocked by the northern shoreline of the Zhenhai Port Area and flows eastward. After bypassing the Waiyou Mountain, it flows into the Jintang Waterway. The northern shoreline of the Zhenhai Port Area and the Waiyou Mountain form a deep pool of erosion due to the outward extension similar to the head of a butyl dam. However, human activities in recent decades have brought about significant changes to the topography and water flow conditions of this sea area :(1) In order to build the Zhenhai Port, a guide dike about 3,186 meters long was cast and constructed from Zhaobao Mountain to Youshan Mountain in August 1975, cutting off the water and sediment exchange between the shoreline and the outside of the Yongjiang River estuary. After the completion of the guide dike, the mouth of the Yongjiang River was moved down to the vicinity of Waiyou Mountain, and obvious siltation and subsidence occurred in the Liuyin area on the west side of the guide dike. (2) In the 1980s, about 2,000 hectares (30,000 mu) of land were spread around the beach in Houzhen, causing the shoreline to move outward. 0 km. (3) In 2001, for the construction of Berth 18 of Zhenhai Port, approximately 40 hectares (600 mu) of land was enclosed behind it for tank farming. This directly altered the topography and water flow conditions of the Deep pool's adjacent sea area and pushed the shoreline outward by 250 meters. The length of the outlying mountain body in the water area has been shortened from 300 meters to 50 meters, significantly weakening the effect of compressing and diverting the ebb and flow within the water area, resulting in further siltation of the deep pools in the outlying mountain. (4) From 2002 to 2004, the coastal section of Zhenhai was surrounded and coated with approximately 1,933 hm 2(2). (90,000 mu), pushing the shoreline outward by approximately 0. 4 to 1 7 km;  The construction of the Xin Hong Kou embankment, which began in 2005 and was completed in July 2008, pushed the coastline of the Xin Hong Port section outward by 1. 3 to 1 8 km;  The Jintang Bridge was built in April 2006. (5) The siltation environment on the southern shore of Hangzhou Bay is also a major backdrop for the siltation of deep mountain pools during outings. To sum up, the range of the deep trough in the sea area remains basically unchanged due to the relatively stable Jintang waterway. However, local erosion and siltation in the sea area are closely related to human activities. Human activities such as land reclamation from the sea and construction of guide embankments directly lead to a reduction in tidal volume. The dynamic effect of water flow in this water area gradually weakens. This weakening trend of dynamic effect of water flow is bound to cause the water area to develop in the direction of siltation. The local encirclement and coating significantly weakened the rockhead effect of the deep pools in the outlying mountains, causing further siltation of the deep pools. When the tidal current points towards the shore, it is conducive to the transportation of silt and sand towards the shore, and it is easy for them to deposit on the shore. The siltation and swelling of the Zhenhai coast and the deep pools in the outlying mountains are mainly related to these three factors.

Since 1963, the Outer mountain Deep Pool has accumulated about 19 meters of silt. It can be roughly divided as follows: Among them, about 3 before 1984 The 3-meter siltation was triggered by the construction of a guide embankment in 1975. From 1984 to 1999, approximately 9 The 0 m siltation was caused by the reclamation of 2,000 hm 2 (30,000 mu) in the coastal section of Zhenhai. From 1999 to 2005, approximately 7 meters of siltation was formed by the reclamation of 40 hm 2 (600 mu) behind Berth 18, the construction of Berth 18, and the 1,933 hm 2 along the Zhenhai coast section. Caused by the enclosure of 90,000 mu of land. The accumulated 0 from 2005 to 2008 5 meters was caused by the reclamation at Xin Hongkou. Since July 2008, there has been no siltation and backflushing for about 1. 0 meters. This is estimated to be related to the erosion on the surface of the gray turtle ocean bed during this period in the larger environment. It should be noted that although the siltation of deep mountain pools during outings is caused by various human activities both near and far, the mechanisms of siltation are not exactly the same. The distant coating is mainly caused by the reduced flow rate leading to siltation. The 40 hm 2 (600 mu) enclosure on the upstream side of the nearby outlying mountain has caused siltation due to shortening the length of the protrusion and significantly weakening the rockhead effect of the outlying mountain. Although the reclamation area of the latter is not large, the weight of the impact of the siltation of the deep mountain pools in the outer area is greater than that of the reclamation in the distant areas

In China's coastal ports, high-pile wharfs generally suffer from siltation on the bank slopes and cracking and damage to the pile foundations, which seriously threaten the safe operation of the wharfs. To effectively implement maintenance measures such as bank slope dredging and pile foundation repair, the primary task is to reveal the cracking mechanism of wharf pile foundations under the condition of bank slope siltation. The current situation of siltation on the bank slopes of 16 major coastal ports in Jiangsu and Zhejiang regions and the cracking and damage of pile foundations at 7 typical wharves were investigated. Combined with specific projects, a finite element model of the interaction between the siltation bank slope and the pile foundation of the wharf was established, and the force deformation characteristics of the pile-soil system were analyzed. The plastic deformation state of the pile foundation was determined by the combination value of internal forces, which was basically consistent with the actual position of the cracking axis of the wharf pile foundation, verifying the rationality of the modeling. A total of seven factors, including the engineering geological conditions of the bank slope and the structural parameters of the high-pile wharf, were selected for orthogonal experiments. The range analysis method was used to explore the sensitivity of each factor to the internal forces of the pile foundation. The results show that in the Marine environment, sediment continuously accumulates beneath and behind the pile foundation wharf, and the continuous deformation of the Marine soft soil layer causes passive pile problems: under the condition of siltation on the bank slope, the soil beneath the wharf has a significant tendency to move towards the sea side, and the adjacent pile body is squeezed to produce a large horizontal displacement, resulting in a sharp increase in the axial force and bending moment of the pile foundation, which in turn induces the top of some pile foundations or the areas with the maximum displacement to exceed the plastic deformation limit. During the siltation process, the additional internal force on the shore inclined piles at the rear of the wharf is greater, making them more prone to cracking and damage. Under the condition of siltation on the bank slope, the axial force at the top of the wharf pile and the bending moment are relatively sensitive to the structural parameters and the slope of the bank slope.

This paper investigates the siltation on the bank slopes of coastal port wharfs in Jiangsu and Zhejiang regions. In view of the current situation of pile foundation damage, the PLAXIS 2D software was applied to establish the silt. The finite element model of the interaction between the bank accumulation slope and the wharf pile foundation was analyzed. The force-deformation characteristics of the silted bank slope - wharf pile foundation system have been revealed. The cracking mechanism of wharf pile foundations under the condition of siltation on the bank slope was explored. The sensitivity of the internal force influencing factors of the wharf pile foundation is studied as follows. (1) The phenomenon of siltation on the banks of ports along the coasts of Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces occurs frequently, and in some cases. The siltation height of the wharf exceeds 10 meters, and the slope of the bank after siltation is relatively large, mainly. The siltation area is beneath and along the rear edge of the wharf. Wharf pile foundations are mostly large. For diameter prestressed pipe piles or square piles, damage is generally manifested as tilting or. Circumferential cracking often occurs in inclined piles facing the shore. (2) Under the condition of siltation on the bank slope, the volume of sediment at the top of the slope and its own. The weight has been increasing year by year, causing continuous horizontal deformation of the slope body. Meanwhile. The pile body is compressed by the surrounding soil, causing horizontal displacement, which in turn leads to. The bending moment and axial force of the pile shaft increase sharply. When the internal force of the pile body exceeds its bearing capacity. When subjected to force, the top of some pile foundations or the areas with the maximum horizontal displacement reach plasticity. The deformed state eventually led to the cracking of the wharf pile foundation. (3) Siltation on the bank slope affects the rear pile foundation of the high-pile wharf structure, especially. Especially the impact of the inclined piles towards the shore is more significant. During the process of siltation, towards. When the additional internal force of the inclined pile on the shore increases, plastic deformation occurs first, followed by the slope. The continuous silting of the top sediment gradually increases the additional internal forces of some straight piles and sea-inclined piles, and the structure of the pile body may also crack and break. (4) Plastic deformation of wharf pile foundations under siltation loads. The shape follows the compression and bending failure mechanism. The plastic deformation at the top of the pile is at the end of the component. The compression-bending failure is affected by the bending moment, while the pile shaft part is the lateral load of the soil. Bending failure caused by loading. In two types of bending failure modes. Below, cracking always occurs on the pulled side of the pile body. (5) The results of the sensitivity analysis show that the internal force at the top of the pile affects the structure. The sensitivity of parameters is relatively high, and the sensitivity of axial force to the frame spacing is particularly high. First, the bending moment is most sensitive to the diameter of the pile. In the engineering geological conditions of the bank slope, The slope has a significant impact on the internal force at the top of the pile, while the velocity of silt return and cohesion. The influence of force is relatively small. Regarding the siltation phenomenon of coastal port slopes and wharfs in Jiangsu and Zhejiang regions. For the problem of pile foundation cracking, it is recommended to carry out damage early warning monitoring and pay close attention. The top of the inclined piles along the rear of the wharf towards the shore. At the same time, to prevent siltation on the bank slopes. If it continuously causes adverse effects on the structure of the wharf, the silt should be cleared regularly. Sediment, reducing the volume and self-weight of sediment, and lowering the horizontal variation of the slope. Shape. In the process of wharf design and construction, the structure needs to be reasonably considered. Parameters, especially the spacing of the frame and the diameter of the piles. Although at PLAXIS. In the middle two-dimensional model, the pile spacing parameters can be set and adjusted within one. To a certain extent, it reflects the three-dimensional situation, but takes into account the high-pile quay - shore. The complexity of the slope interaction system should be addressed by establishing a three-dimensional finite system in the future. Conduct more comprehensive and in-depth research on the meta-model.

In slope embankments and inclined top pile wharfs, pile foundations not only pass on the partial load but also restrict the lateral deformation of the bank slope, which is a typical passive pile problem of shore-slope interaction [1]. Under the dynamic environment of marine currents and the water-blocking conditions of hydraulic structures, after the completion of high-pile wharfs, they usually remain in the phenomenon of siltation [2]. Gao Zhiyong et al. [3] through the trend of sediment transport comprehensive analysis through methods such as models, field tests, and observations of bank sections found that due to factors such as waves and tides, siltation at the front of the wharf reached the height of 3 meters, and the height of the south side is even 6 meters. Ni Yunlin et al. [4] collected Beilun topographic maps of water depth in multiple typical years of the port area and established digital elevation models. Research has found that the shape of the shoreline changes due to the construction of the wharf berths, and the underwater shore slope has undergone an adjustment process of scouring and siltation. Jin et al. [5] and Ying et al. [6] respectively utilized the FVCOM model and MIKE21 model to study the impact of tidal changes before and after the construction of the wharf on silt accumulation. The influence law and results show that after the wharf is completed, the water flow speed in the project area drops significantly, causing siltation on the underwater shore slope. The characteristics of the siltation load and its cumulative effect cause continuous deformation of the soil on the bank slope, resulting in the group pile foundation beneath the beams and slabs structure having been in a passive working state all the time, which has adversely affected the service performance of the wharf pile foundation.

In recent years, the service performance of wharf pile foundations under siltation on the bank slope has gradually drawn attention. Thomas et al. [7] explored the possibility of deep slope failure after siltation of the bank slope, believing that the lateral load exerted on the piles by the shore slope moving towards the sea is the fundamental cause of the deformation of the wharf. Shi Bin [8] analyzed the settlement of inclined steel pipe piles at a certain high-pile wharf on soft soil foundation and concluded that the negative frictional force generated by the siltation of the bank slope on the pile is one of the factors causing deformation of pile foundations. Li Rongqing et al. [9] established a three-dimensional finite element model of the high-pile wharf-siltation bank slope to study the influence law of sediment siltation on the bending moment of the pile foundation of the high-pile wharf. The results showed that the additional earth pressure caused by bank slope siltation leads to the maximum bending moment occurring at the top of the pile foundation. Liu Yanzhong et al. [10] studied the internal force changes of the quay fork piles under various loads, suggesting that the siltation load on the bank slope is the main cause of the damage to the inclined piles along the fork piles behind the pile platform towards the shore. Zhang Zhenchao [11] analyzed the maximum principal stress and internal force distribution of pile foundations under different siltation depths, finding that when the accumulation is greater than 3 meters, the tensile stress is greatest at a distance of 2 meters from the top of the pile, causing structural cracking. Zhang Dong et al. [12] established overall structures coupled with the high-pile wharf and the shore slope soil, respectively creating section models and single-row frame models to study the deformation law of wharf pile foundations during the siltation process. They concluded that the siltation of the bank slope has a significant impact on the deformation and displacement of the front edge of the pile foundation position, with the top of the pile being the most unfavorable position for structural stress. Zhang Luhua et al. [13] used numerical simulation methods to analyze the influence of different mud surface heights on the pile foundation of the wharf and predicted the most unfavorable mud surface elevation line.

For the lower and rear slopes of high-pile wharf structures in coastal ports, previous research on siltation phenomena has mainly focused on evaluating the internal force variation law and horizontal displacement conditions of the wharf pile foundation during the siltation process. There has been little analysis of the adverse effects of siltation on the pile foundation of the wharf, and the sensitivity of influencing factors on the internal forces of pile foundations under siltation conditions has not been thoroughly investigated. Researchers have not delved deeply into the key issue of how siltation of the bank slope causes cracking and damage to the wharf pile foundation. For this reason, this article conducted a survey on the current situation of siltation on the slopes of major coastal ports in Jiangsu and Zhejiang regions and typical cracking and damage of wharf pile foundations under siltation conditions. On this basis, combined with the actual high-pile wharf project, a finite element model of the interaction between wharf pile foundation and silted bank slope was established using PLAXIS 2D software. The deformation characteristics of the soil system under force were analyzed, revealing the cracking mechanism of the wharf pile foundation under the condition of bank slope siltation. The research obtained the sensitivity of influencing factors on axial force and bending moment at the top of the wharf pile. The research results can provide a theoretical basis for addressing the cracking restoration and prevention of wharf pile foundations, which is conducive to ensuring the safe and stable operation of coastal ports, extending their service life, and reducing maintenance costs, thus possessing long-term engineering application value.

The trend of siltation on the bank slopes of typical wharf P and typical wharf N is obvious. This work is more representative in the issue of damaged pile foundations and has detailed engineering geological data and underwater measurement data which are reliable. Therefore, based on these two wharves as examples, this study explores the cracking mechanism of wharf pile foundations under the condition of siltation on the bank slope. The pier pile foundation - siltation bank slope finite element model of interaction was established by applying PLAXIS 2D software. The upper beam and slab structure of the wharf adopts the elastic constitutive model, while the pile foundation structure under the wharf adopts the elastoplastic constitutive model. For the construction model, the soil on the bank slope is constructed in accordance with the Mohr-Coulomb strength criterion using an elastoplastic constitutive model. Using the Embedded beam row structure unit to define the group pile foundation can reasonably simulate the displacement and coupling changes of the pile-soil system, thereby accurately reflecting the force state and deformation characteristics of the wharf pile foundation in actual engineering. The Embedded beam row structure unit is composed of a beam unit and a contact unit, among which the contact unit can simulate the contact relationship between the pile and the soil. In the finite element model, the left and right boundaries of the bank slope constrain the horizontal displacement, while the bottom boundary is set with fixed constraints, and the top boundary (surface and mud surface) is free. The typical P model of the wharf selects the horizontal square within the soil range of the bank slope with a dimension of 270 meters in the horizontal direction, a depth of 80 meters, and the elevation of the quay panel at 4 meters. Engineering projects were carried out on the wharf in 2008, 2012 and 2015 with quality surveys involving measuring the underwater mud surface elevation to obtain the siltation state of the bank slopes for each year as shown in Figure 1. The active siltation soil layer was stimulated one by one using the life-and-death unit method. The initial elevation of the mud surface at the front of the wharf is -12 meters, and after dredging at the front end, the designed elevation was -17.5 meters. According to the drilling report, the lithology, physical and mechanical properties, and the age of formation of the exposed strata show that the soil within the exploration depth of the site is divided into 8 engineering geological layers (Table 3). The spacing of each frame in the structural section of the wharf model is 10.5 meters. Each frame consists of 10 pile foundations, and the axis numbers from left to right are A to K as shown in Table 4 for material parameters. When specifying the weight of the pile foundation, note that the Embedded beam row unit itself in PLAXIS 2D does not occupy any volume but covers the soil unit, thus requiring to subtract the specific gravity of the soil from the specific gravity of the pile base material. The typical N-model structure section of the wharf has a frame spacing of 8.5 meters, and each frame contains 11 pile foundations. The axis numbers from left to right are A to L, as shown in Figure 2, with material parameters presented in Table 4. The soil type of the bank slope selection covers 140 meters in the horizontal direction, 74 meters in depth, with the elevation of the wharf panel at 4.6 meters. The designed elevation of the mud surface at the front of the wharf is -14.5 meters. The soil within the exploration depth of the venue is divided into 11 engineering geological layers, specifically detailed in Table 5.

Many coastal sediments are formed by the rapid accumulation of sand, which has a loose structure and is relatively easy to liquefy. This article introduces the method for analyzing the liquefaction hazard of this kind of sediment and applies it to Hong Kong Island. Take the alluvial deposits of San Diego Bay as an example. Make corresponding analyses and evaluations on the properties of this kind of sediment and its possibility of liquefaction. Then, these are linked to the analysis results revealed by the earthquake to estimate the liquefaction probability of the site.

There are many alluvial deposits along the coast and at ports in California, such as San Diego's Harbour Island and Typhoon Shelter, including several airports and many major land extensions for commercial and recreational purposes. After these alluvial deposits were formed, the possibility of their liquefaction was not recognized. For the site under given seismic conditions, the existing analytical methods can evaluate the safety limits of these deposits. This article takes the alluvial deposits of the San Diego Bay seaport as an example to illustrate the evaluation method of liquefaction hazard. To evaluate the conditions of the deep soil, the reliable data obtained from the upper boreholes and the relevant conditions of the sediment structure were sorted out and analyzed. Through the study of the earthquake history, regional geology and seismic conditions in the southern region of California. Identify the main earthquakes that have an impact on this area. At the University of California, Berkeley, a semi-empirical approach was adopted to develop and evaluate the liquefaction sensitivity of soil. The analysis results revealed by earthquakes were linked to the liquefaction sensitivity of seaport islands to assess the probability of liquefaction over the next 50 years. The method discussed in this paper can be used to evaluate the probability of the liquefaction risk of sediment in earthquake-active areas and coastal ports.

Alluvial deposits consist of medium to fine sand (SP) and fine sand (SP-SM) to fine sand (SM). Among them are scattered shell fragments. It is also interspersed with scattered gravel lenses and small lenses made of silt and clay. The average thickness of the alluvial deposits is 7 meters. According to the revised California sample specification, by measuring the penetration resistance of each type of soil, they are classified into the following types: homogeneous sand (SP) 5-38, fine sand (SP-SM) 4-35, silty sand (SM) 5-39, silty sand to silty soil (SM-ML) 1-6, silty soil (ML) 1-5

The bay sediments are Holocene bay sediments. Except for some areas where the sediment was re-migrated due to looseness, these sediments almost covered most of the seabed of San Diego Bay. These sediments are composed of thin layers of organic soft sand (ML) and clay (CL) interbedded with fine sand (SM and SP-SM) mixed with shell fragments. The average thickness of the sediment in the bay is 3 meters. The penetration impact numbers of soil layers were determined using the modified California sample specification as follows: 3-42 for sandy soil layers (SP-SM and SM), 4-6 for siltstone soil layers (SM-ML and ML), and 1-3 for siltstone layers (Cl-ml and CL)

In this study, the reliable data obtained from drilling on Hong Kong Island and the relevant information about the stratum structure were all collated and analyzed to identify the state of the underground soil. The impact data of soil penetration resistance were analyzed, and the cumulative distribution curves were plotted, thereby obtaining the corrected median of each of the six soil groups 𝑁1N 1. The evaluation revealed by the Hong Kong Island earthquake has resulted in a map indicating the probability of all faults identified as having an impact on the site earthquakes exceeding the peak of ground acceleration over a 50-year period. The other graph shows the average number of years for exceeding the given critical peak of foundation acceleration in the peak of foundation acceleration. The boundary curves established by Seed and Idriss for evaluating the liquefaction potential of certain sites were utilized. By converting the periodic stress ratio into the peak value of the critical foundation acceleration, the site characteristic curve of Hong Kong Island was established. Link the analysis results revealed by the earthquake at this site with the possibility of sand liquefaction on Hong Kong Island, and predict the probability of liquefaction over a 50-year period. As shown in Table 2, the research results indicate that for different data combinations, the probability of liquefaction of the site over a 50-year period varies greatly. If the data of the Sheraton Schmi site is analyzed separately, the probability range is 0.25 to 0.55. If the data is sourced from places other than Sheraton, it can be considered that these data represent all areas of Hong Kong Island, including Sheraton. At this point, the probability range decreases to 0.13 to 0.26. If all the data from various places are combined, the probability range is 0.17 to 0.43. This is a conservative assumption for most islands, as Sheraton data accounts for a relatively high proportion and has a significant impact on the analysis results. Figure 8 shows the impact of the peak acceleration of the sedimentary foundation in the bay of Hong Kong Island on the corrected penetration resistance. It should be noted that this study includes some assumed data based on the corrected penetration resistance data. In addition, certain approximations and assumptions are made in the prediction of seismic activity. The final result was quantitatively processed by probability analysis.

For the upper section sketch of Hong Kong Island, the average K of alluvial deposits was calculated to be 0.84, and the K of bay sediments was calculated to be 0.98. Use these values to convert the vertical coordinate of Figure 5 into the peak ground acceleration of each layer of sediment. When the ground acceleration exceeds the critical value, some form of liquefaction will occur. The liquefaction potential curve unique to the Hong Kong Island site can be represented by the shape shown in Figure 7. This figure shows the relationship between the critical foundation acceleration of alluvial deposits and the corrected penetration resistance. Figure 8 is the relationship diagram regarding the sediment in the bay. Use formulas (3) and (4) as well as the 10 corrected standard penetration impact numbers in Table 2 𝑁₁ Then the liquefaction probability of alluvial deposits on Hong Kong Island and sediments in bays can be calculated. And for each median 𝑁₁ It is obtained by using the average magnitudes of eight different earthquakes in Figure 4. For each selected magnitude of earthquake, the curves in Figures 7 and 8 are used to obtain them 𝑎_cr Or the peak of the lowest ground acceleration that causes liquefaction. Referring to the appropriate magnitude curve in Figure 4, it can be used to correspond to each 𝑁₁ Eight values 𝑎_cr Obtain the average number of years leading to liquefaction events 𝜆₀. Then, add up the 8 groups of values using formula (3) to obtain the result 𝜆₁. 𝜆₁ Substitute it into Formula (4) to obtain the assumption corresponding to the given within 50 years 𝑁₁ The probability of value liquefaction. In this study, the data of 39 boreholes on the Bay Island were analyzed. The first step is to streamline the materials. Considering that clay, silt sand and silt sand are not easy to liquefy, the impact number obtained from them is deleted. Only take into account the impact numbers from powdery to pure sand and loose powdery sand. In the liquefaction analysis, there are a total of 91 shock point data available for utilization. The geotechnical test report indicates that in 39 boreholes, an improved California sampler (with an outer diameter of 6.4cm and an inner diameter of 4.9cm) was used, and a 63.5kg impact hammer was applied, with a drop distance of 76cm. Based on the common local experience in San Diego, the conversion factor is taken as 0.8. Convert the impact number obtained with the improved California sampler to the equivalent standard penetration test impact number. In addition, in accordance with the recommendations of Seed and Idriss (1981), three aspects of correction were carried out when measuring the penetration impact number. During the liquefaction analysis, the correction values were adopted. The corrected values included: overload pressure, drill pipe length and silt content. The depth correction values of the data from 91 impact points are shown in Figure 6.

The Pleistocene sediments located beneath the bay sediments have the following characteristics: interbedded medium-dense to dense sand (SP) and silty to silt sand (SM and SC). Among them are randomly distributed hard to hard sandy clay (CH) lens bodies. Pleistocene sediments are not sensitive to liquefaction.
